text: This report is a revised and expanded version of an internal memorandum titled "Preliminary Report on Results of MSRE Zero-Power Experiments," issued in the summer of 1965, immediately following the completion of these experiments.

The program includes the initial critical $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ loading, the control-rod calibration (period-differential worth and rod drop-integral worth measurements), determinations of the reactivity loss due to fuel circulation, the "static" reactivity coefficients of excess $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ concentration and isothermal core temperature, the fuel salt temperature reactivity coefficient, the pressure effects on reactivity, and a series of system dynamics tests (frequency response, transient flow, and neutron flux noise measurements).
These measurements, carried out in June 1965, form much of the experimental baseline for current evaluation of the nuclear operation at full power.
A program of zero-power nuclear experiments, including the initial critical experiment, was conducted on the Molten-Salt Reactor Experiment (MSRE) in June 1965.

With the initial critical concentration established, more $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ was added to the circulating loop in increments to permit the attainment of criticality with the salt circulating and with various control-rod configurations.

Measurements of the critical control-rod configurations as a function of uranium concentration, both with and without fuel circulation, provided information about the $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ concentration coefficient of reactivity, the effect of circulation on reactivity, and control-rod shadowing effects.
At several fixed $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ concentrations, the reactor system temperature was varied to provide data on the isothermal temperature coefficient of reactivity.
It also served to establish the basepoint from which the $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ additions necessary to reach the operating concentration could be made with confidence.
This mixture of salt was then circulated for 10 days at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ while the sampler-enricher was tested and 18 samples were analyzed to establish the initial composition.
The critical experiment then consisted of adding enriched uranium in increments to bring the $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ concentration up to the critical point.
Nuclear instrumentation for the experiment consisted of two fission chambers, two $\mathrm{BF}_3$ chambers, and an $^{241}\mathrm{Am - }^{242}\mathrm{Cm - }$ Be source, located as shown in Fig.
The fuel salt itself also constituted a neutron source, due to reaction of alpha particles from $^{234}\mathrm{U}$ with beryllium and fluorine.
The latter method was limited to $85\mathrm{g}^{235}\mathrm{U}$ per capsule, only 0.0012 of the expected critical loading.
Therefore the bulk of the $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ was added in four additions to the drain tank.
After the third addition, with $64.54\mathrm{kg}^{235}\mathrm{U}$ in the salt, the projected critical loading was $70.0 \pm 0.5\mathrm{kg}^{235}\mathrm{U}$ .

(The l-in. $\mathrm{BF}_3$ chamber, located in the thermal shield, whose count rates extrapolated to a higher value was known to be strongly affected by neutrons coming directly from the source.) The fourth addition was intended to bring the loading to about $1\mathrm{kg}$ below the critical point.
After $4.38\mathrm{kg}$ of $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ was added, the count rates showed the loading was within $0.8\mathrm{kg}$ of critical when the rods were withdrawn and circulation was stopped.
In the final stage, enriching capsules were added through the pump bowl to bring the loading up 85 g at a time.
With the reactor within $0.2\%$ 8k/k of critical, slight variations in temperature caused considerable changes in multiplication.
At approximately 6:00 p.m., June 1, 1965, the reactor reached the critical point, with two rods at full withdrawal and the other inserted 0.03 of its worth.
The $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ loading was 69.6 kg.
The MSRE fuel mixture has an inherent source of neutrons produced by the interaction of alpha particles (primarily from $^{234}\mathrm{U}$ ) with the beryllium and fluorine.

Count-rate determinations with and without the external neutron source in place, under otherwise identical conditions, showed that the internal source supplied 0.03 to 0.05 as many neutrons to the core as the external source.
The external source at that time had an absolute intensity of $1 \times 10^{8}$ neutrons/sec.
1), some distance from the reactor vessel, only a small fraction of these neutrons are effective in reaching the core.

If this fraction were $10\%$ ,* the effective external source contribution would be $1 \times 10^{7}$ neutrons/sec, and thus the internal source strength would be in the range of $3 \times 10^{5}$ to $5 \times 10^{5}$ neutrons/sec.
The calculated intensity of the internal source was within this range. $^{1}$
The observed $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ concentrations, however, are obtained in the first instance on a weight basis, either from inventory records or from chemical analyses.
The amounts of $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ and salt weighed into the system gave a $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ mass fraction of $1.414 \pm 0.005$ wt % at the time of the initial criticality.
The chemical analyses during the precritical operation and the zero-power experiments gave uranium mass fractions which were 0.985 of the "book" fractions.
Applying this bias to the book fraction at criticality gave an "analytical" $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ mass fraction of 1.393 wt %.
On a statistical basis, the uncertainty in the mass fractions obtained from chemical analyses is about $\pm 0.007$ wt %.

(During the initial fill operations, $^7\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2}$ flush salt was admitted to the fuel circulating system.) Experience with drain-flush-fill cycles obtained from MSRE operation subsequent to the zero-power experiments has indicated that the fuel salt would have been diluted by $20 \pm 10$ kg of flush salt.
If we assume that this amount of dilution occurred, the corrected value of the book mass fraction of $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ would be $1.408 \pm 0.007$ wt%.

The density of the fuel salt at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ was determined after the uranium was added to the fuel drain tank, using pre-calibrated drain tank weigh cells and salt level probes within the tanks.
The average of four measurements was 145.1 lb/ft³, with a maximum deviation of 1.1 lb/ft³.
This measured density, computed from externally measured weights and the volume between the level probes within the tanks was 140.6 lb/ft³ at 1200°F.

In comparing the observed and calculated critical concentration of $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ , a small temperature correction should be applied to the salt density given above, since the core temperature at the time of criticality was $1181^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ instead of $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Based on a fractional change in density of $-1.2 \times 10^{-4} / {}^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ (see discussion in Sec.
4.1), the density at $1181^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ would have been $145.3 \pm 1.0 \, \text{lb/ft}^3$ .

Finally, corrections must also be applied to the calculated critical concentration, both for the lower temperature and the fact that one rod was at 46.6 in., compared to the reference conditions of $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and all three rods at maximum withdrawal, 51 in.

The calculated $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ concentration for criticality at the reference conditions was 32.87 g/liter; corrected to the actual conditions, using measured values of the temperature coefficient of reactivity and the control-rod worth increment (see later sections), it is 32.77 g/liter.
This "predicted" value is compared with "observed" $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ concentrations in Table 1.
Concentrations corresponding to both the book mass fraction, corrected for the flush salt dilution, and the analytical mass fraction, described above, are listed in Table 1.
Comparison of Critical $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ Concentrations (1181°F, pump off; 0.08% sk/k rod poisoning)

The addition of $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ beyond the minimum critical loading had a twofold objective: to end with enough excess reactivity to permit operation at full power and in the process to make measurements which could be analyzed to give control-rod worth and various reactivity coefficients.
The final amount of $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ was to be enough to be critical at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ with the fuel stationary and one rod fully inserted.

The general method was to add $85\mathrm{g}^{235}\mathrm{U}$ at a time through the sampler-enricher, after the addition determine the new critical rod position, and at longer intervals do other experiments.
Following the initial critical experiment, another eight capsules were required before the reactor could be made critical at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ with the fuel pump running.

Once this $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ concentration had been reached, the critical position of the control rod to be calibrated (designated as the regulating rod) was measured after addition of each capsule, with the fuel pump running.
This went on until a total of 87 capsules had been added.
Three times during the course of additions of $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ (after 30, 65, and 87 capsules) rod-drop effects were observed.

Each curve is a qualitative graphical description of the change in the static reactivity* as a function of regulating-rod position, with the other two rods withdrawn to their upper limits (position 0).
The various curves represent different total loadings of $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ , increasing in the direction shown by the arrow.
The static reactivity, $\rho_{\mathrm{s}}$ , corresponding to each specific rod position and $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ loading is defined by the equation:

4 that, if the reactivity equivalent of the $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ addition is known, a direct means of calibration of the reactivity worth of the regulating rod is provided, simply by relating the critical position of the rod (solid points shown as examples in Fig.
4) to the $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ loading.

Alternatively, calibration of the rod by independent experiments provides an empirical determination of the reactivity worth of the additional $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ , or the concentration coefficient of reactivity.
4 a typical measurement of the stable period corresponds to a motion from the critical position upward and to the left along the short segment marked (p).
4 by the segment marked (d), extending from the initial critical position into the subcritical region.

Because the reactivity worth of the rods is affected by the $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ concentration in the core, one finds that $\rho_{\mathrm{sl}} < |\rho_{\mathrm{so}}|$ , or equivalently, that the curves representing different fuel loadings are not exactly parallel.

Although the $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ loading was continually being increased during the course of these experiments, it is useful for purposes of consistency to interpret the combined reactivity measurements, over the whole range of rod movement, on the basis of a single mass of $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ .

Theoretical calculations of the rod costs, summarized later, were used to determine the effects of the $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ concentration on total cost, and these corrections were used as an aid in normalizing the experimental reactivity measurements to a single $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ loading.
The rod being calibrated was first adjusted to make the reactor critical at about 10 w.
The rod was then inserted to bring the power back to 10 w and the measurement was repeated at a somewhat shorter stable period.
The stable period was determined by averaging the slopes of the two curves (which usually agreed within about $2\%$ ).
Periods observed were generally in the range of 30 to 150 sec.

Prior to pulling the rod for each period measurement, the attempt was made to hold the power level at 10 w for at least 3 min, in an effort to help insure initial equilibrium of the delayed neutron precursors.

As discussed above, theoretical corrections have been applied to these measurements to put them all on the basis of one $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ concentration, arbitrarily chosen as the initial critical concentration at the beginning of the rod-calibration experiments.
3.8 indicated that the static reactivity worth of a single rod is reduced by nearly $9\%$ of its total worth for the total addition of $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ made during the course of these experiments.
5 increase linearly with $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ concentration, up to 1.087 for the measurements made near the final concentration (corresponding to the points between 1 and 2 in. withdrawal).
5, because the differential worth is the ratio of the increment in reactivity to the increment in rod withdrawal, each of which is subject to experimental error.
5 from the curve is about $2.8 \times 10^{-4}\%$ $\mathrm{sk / k / in}$ , or $\sim 0.7\%$ of the mean differential worth; the maximum deviation of a single point was $8.7\%$ .
the precision of determining the rod position was about $\pm 0.01$ in.
Figure 6 shows a curve of the magnitude of rod reactivity vs position, which is the integral of the differential worth curve in Fig.
An integral worth curve is also shown which is normalized to the final $235\mathrm{U}$ concentration.
This latter is simply the first curve reduced by a factor of 1.087.

The $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ concentration coefficient of reactivity is given by the ratio of the change in reactivity to the fractional change in $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ concentration, or circulating mass, as a result of a small addition.
7 at any particular concentration, multiplied by that concentration.

The value of $(\delta\mathrm{k}/\mathrm{k}) / (\delta\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{m})$ obtained from the experimental curves was 0.223, which was very nearly independent of $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ mass over the range shown in Fig.
The theoretically calculated value of this quantity was 0.248 for the approach to the initial critical mass, and 0.234 for the average during the excess uranium additions.

2 and 3) were inserted into the core.
3 held fixed in the fully withdrawn position, and the effect of inserting rods
2 and 3 as a bank, that is, with their tips at identical elevations.
Figure 8 shows the data obtained from these experiments.
Each experiment was terminated at the 45-deg line, where the tips of all three control rods are at equal insertions.

To the critical position of the regulating rod at the start of each experiment with a given $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ loading, there corresponds an equivalent excess reactivity, relative to the reference conditions.

8 represents control rod positions at conditions of criticality, it follows that the curve pairs corresponding to a given $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ loading represent various shim-regulating-rod combinations which are equal in excess reactivity worth.

(This relative invariance of the shape of the worth vs position curves is supported by theoretical calculations.) The conversion of the reactivity measurements in the manner described above is summarized in Table 3.

Early in the theoretical studies of MSRE physics, the effect of the change in delayed-neutron precursor distributions due to circulation had been predicted to be $-0.30\% \delta \mathrm{k} / \mathrm{k}$ .
It was expected that another $-0.2\% \delta \mathrm{k} / \mathrm{k}$ might be present due to entrained helium bubbles in the circulating salt.

By extending this analysis to include the contribution of the delayed neutrons emitted while the fuel is in the upper and lower plenums, just outside the graphite region, a reactivity difference of $0.222\% \text{sk/k}$ was calculated from theoretical analysis.

The three scalers were stacked together in a vertical array, and a rapid-action camera was used to photograph the records on the three scalers simultaneously approximately once every second, starting a few seconds before the scram and ending about 30 sec after the scram.

In these experiments, the position of the fission chambers was adjusted to obtain an initial count rate at criticality of $\sim 30,000$ counts/sec, which was low enough to result in quite small counting-rate losses due to dead-time.

We have also used an algebraic representation of these experimental curves, obtained by a least-squares curve-fitting procedure.[11]
The results of the rod-drop experiments made after additions of 30, 65, and 87 capsules of enriching salt are shown in Figs.
11, 12, and 13 respectively.
In the case of the two-rod experiments, results obtained after 65 and 87 capsules appear to be slightly anomalous with respect to the experiment with the same rod group after 30 capsules.
Multiplication of the magnitude of the negative reactivity insertion by a factor of about 1.05 brings the calculated and experimental curves for these experiments into better agreement.
Here the analysis of the experiment where rod No. l was scrambled after addition of 30 capsules is reproduced from Fig.
11; the theoretical curves are added which correspond to an increase and a decrease of $5\%$ of the total magnitude of negative reactivity insertion.
For this particular experiment, this corresponds to an increment of $0.007\% \text{8k/k}$ in the magnitude of reactivity.

With the largest apparent differences occurring in the experiments involving the scram of two rods, the results of all these experiments were within the $5\%$ band of self-consistency with the rod calibration results obtained from the differential-worth measurements.

text: The programs which were used to calculate the expected neutron energy spectrum over most of the reactor core were the GAM-II and the THERMOS codes. $^{12,13}$ The former calculates the epithermal energy spectrum of the neutron flux (the flux above energies where thermal motion of the moderator nuclei exerts a strong influence on the neutron spectrum).

section: 3.8 Comparison of Measurements with Theoretical Analysis of Control-Rod Worth
text: In each of these programs, the actual energy spectrum is approximated by a finite number of energy groups (99 groups in GAM-II spanning the interval 15 Mev to 0.414 ev, and 30 groups in THERMOS, spanning the interval 0 to 0.876 ev).

The effect of temperature on reactivity was measured in three separate experiments in which we adjusted the electric heaters to change the system temperature slowly (about $15^{\circ}\mathrm{F/hr}$ ) while we observed the critical position of the regulating rod.
Figure 16 shows the results of the three experiments involving slow changes in temperature.

Here, the magnitude of the reactivity change about the nominal $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ operating point is plotted as a function of the fuel, or system temperature (temperature changes were induced so slowly that the fuel and graphite could be safely assumed to remain in thermal equilibrium).

16 are the quantities of interest, the positions of the curves have been translated parallel to the vertical axis in order that the three curves might be plotted on the same scale and still be distinguished.
The first experiment, with $67.9\mathrm{kg}$ of $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ in the loop gave a line whose slope ranges from $-(6.6$ to $8.3)\times 10^{-5}{}^{\circ}\mathrm{F}^{-1}$ .
At $69.9\mathrm{kg}^{235}\mathrm{U}$ , the experiment gave a straight line with a slope of $-7.24\times 10^{-5}{}^{\circ}\mathrm{F}^{-1}$ .
In the last experiment, at $71.7\mathrm{kg}^{235}\mathrm{U}$ , the slope of the curve above about $1140^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ is $-7.3\times 10^{-5}{}^{\circ}\mathrm{F}^{-1}$ .
Calculations consistent with the models described in the preceding section gave a value of $-8.1 \times 10^{-5} \circ \mathrm{F}^{-1}$ for the total isothermal temperature coefficient of reactivity.
The associated components for the fuel salt and graphite were $-4.1 \times 10^{-5} \circ \mathrm{F}^{-1}$ and $-4.0 \times 10^{-5} \circ \mathrm{F}^{-1}$ , respectively.
These calculations were based on an expansion coefficient of $-1.18 \times 10^{-4} \circ \mathrm{F}^{-1}$ , obtained from an early empirical correlation of temperature with density.

This value was later found to be in good agreement with the expansion coefficient determined from observations of the change in salt level in the MSRE fuel pump bowl with loop temperature (two separate measurements gave $-1.09 \times 10^{-4}$ and $-1.15 \times 10^{-4} \circ \mathrm{F}^{-1}$ ).<sup>3</sup>
The experiment at 71.7 kg $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ shows a lower slope below about $1140^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

This is a photograph of a three-dimensional model* showing the critical position of the regulating rod (vertical axis) as a function of the $235\mathrm{U}$ concentration (horizontal axis, nearly in the plane of the page), and core temperature (depth axis, front-to-back of the model).

The measured critical position of the regulating rod with fuel circulating at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ vs the $235\mathrm{U}$ concentration is represented by the relatively "dense" curve sloping downward to the right.

The reactor power was controlled at 10 w by the flux servo.\* For these experiments, use was made of a Bunker-Ramo 340 on-line digital computer and data logger, installed as part of the MSRE instrumentation.\*\* The output of a thermocouple in the reactor vessel outlet, logged digitally at 1/4-sec intervals, showed a brief increase of 5 to $6^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ as the hot salt first passed.
It then leveled at about $3.5^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ for a few loop transit times before decreasing gradually.

The noise in the analog-to-digital conversion $(\pm 1^{\circ}\mathrm{F})$ limited the accuracy of the measurement, but by taking an average of 50 points during the level period after mixing and before the graphite temperature had time to change significantly, a value was obtained for the increment in fuel temperature.
The resulting coefficient was $-(4.9 \pm 2.3) \times 10^{-5} \circ \mathrm{F}^{-1}$ .
The reactor inlet and outlet temperature thermocouple signals were preamplified and filtered before digitizing, reducing the noise to about $\pm 0.1^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

After heating the stagnant coolant salt about $20^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ hotter than the fuel salt, the coolant pump was started, introducing a hot slug of fuel into the heat exchanger and subsequently into the core.
If the immediate effects could be attributed to the fuel temperature coefficient alone, a value of $-4.7 \pm 0.7) \times 10^{-5} \circ \mathrm{F}^{-1}$ was obtained from this test.
A small fraction of the fuel pump discharge stream (about 50 gpm out of 1250 gpm) is diverted into a spray ring in the gas space in the pump bowl.
The purpose of the spray, or stripper, is to provide contact so that $^{135}\mathrm{Xe}$ in the salt can escape into the gas space, which is continuously purged.

In each of the three tests the loop overpressure was slowly increased from the normal 5 psig to 15 psig and then quickly relieved, through a bypass valve, to a drain tank that had previously been vented to atmospheric pressure.
The third test was performed at an abnormally low pump-bowl level which was obtained by lowering the operating temperature to $1050^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Figure 18 shows the pressure transient and the responses of the regulating control rod, densitometer, and fuel-pump level during the rapid pressure release.
For these conditions, a change of one unit on the densitometer was equivalent to a change of about 0.15 vol % in the circulating void fraction.
Independent evaluations of the void fraction from these three parameters all gave values between $2\%$ and $3\%$ by volume.

The reactor system was analyzed on a theoretical basis,[20] and the tests were designed not only to characterize the present system but also to evaluate the techniques and mathematical models used in the theoretical analysis.
In the pulse tests a control rod was withdrawn $1/2$ in., held there for 3.5 or 7 sec., then returned to its original position.
The rod was located such that $1/2$ -in. travel caused a change in reactivity of about $0.03\%$ .
The rod position signal and flux signal were recorded digitally at 0.25 sec intervals using the BR-340 on-line digital computer.
To circumvent this problem, the flux output signal was filtered with a 50-sec time-constant, high-pass digital filter.

"Practice" tests on a simulated zero-power reactor indicated that reasonably accurate results could be expected from these tests only if the fuel-temperature drift during a test could be held essentially to zero, and if the control rod could be repositioned to its initial location within an accuracy of better than 0.01 in.

The principle advantage of a PRBS in frequency response testing is that its frequency spectrum typically consists of a large number of harmonics of approximately equal size.[22] This means that the response may be analyzed at many frequencies generated in a single test, and the signal-to-noise ratio at these known frequencies is typically very good.
The lowest harmonic radian frequency $\omega_{0}$ and the spacing between harmonics $\Delta \omega$
Sequences of 19 and 63 bits were used in the zero-power tests with bit times of 6.58 and 3.35 sec, respectively.

The 19-bit sequence was generated manually, while the 63-bit test sequence, which was run after the on-line computer was operational, was generated automatically by a special shift-register algorithm.
The rod motion about the average position was adjusted to give a reactivity perturbation of about $\pm 0.015\%$ .
As with the pulse tests, the rod-position and neutron-level signals were recorded digitally at 0.25-sec intervals using the MSRE computer.

These points were obtained by taking the square root of the measured PSD after subtracting the asymptotic high-frequency value of PSD due to noise in the neutron detection process.[25] This computation results in an amplitude-ratio vs frequency curve if one assumes that the input reactivity noise is white, that is, has a constant PSD in the frequency range of interest.

Since the magnitude of the input PSD is unknown, only the relative magnitude of the noise results are available; thus the curves were arbitrarily normalized to the theoretical results at 9 radians/sec.

The results of the noise analysis of the case where bubbles are entrained in the fuel salt showed a substantial increase in PSD in the frequency range of 1 to 10 radians/sec over the no-bubble case, indicating an increase in the input reactivity fluctuation.
